description: The displayed image is an irrelevant UI placeholder button instead of the expected research figure for the low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al) study. It contains the text "Check for updates", a partial circular ring with blue upper segment and yellow lower segment, and a red bookmark-shaped icon inside the ring. No content related to the alloy's microstructure, mechanical properties, anisotropic deformation behavior, phase evolution, or related characterization results is visible in this placeholder image, so no valid scientific information supporting the paper's conclusions can be extracted from this visual content.

This figure presents the pseudo-binary equilibrium phase diagram of the Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al (wt.%) low-density medium manganese steel system, calculated via Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE9 database. The x-axis denotes the weight percent of Al ranging from 0 to 10 wt.%, while the y-axis represents temperature from 500 to 1600 °C. Multiple phase regions are labeled, including liquid phase (L), γ+L, α+L, single austenite (γ), γ+α, γ+θ, γ+α+κ, and α+κ, illustrating phase stability variations with Al content and temperature. A red star marked "Current Study" is plotted at ~4.94 wt.% Al and 850 °C, corresponding to the intercritical annealing condition adopted in this work, which locates in the γ+α two-phase region, verifying that the annealing process can produce the target dual-phase microstructure of austenite and ferrite as designed. The phase diagram also confirms that Al addition promotes ferrite formation in the studied alloy system.

This figure presents in-situ synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) characterization results for a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel. Subfigure (a) shows the HEXRD experimental setup with 60 keV fixed-energy photons, for testing tensile specimens cut along rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Subfigure (b) displays diffraction intensity mappings as a function of azimuth angle (0-360°) and 2-Theta (5-12°) for non-deformed, 30% strained RD, and 30% strained TD samples, with identified FCC austenite (γ) and BCC ferrite (α) diffraction peaks labeled. Subfigure (c) shows azimuth-integrated XRD profiles including experimental data and Rietveld refinement calculated results for the three sample states, with good matching between experimental and calculated values confirming phase identification reliability. Subfigure (d) presents the integrated diffraction profile of non-deformed sample along azimuth angle -5° to 5°, with single-peak fitting separating FCC (green) and BCC (blue) phase peaks for lattice strain calculation. This figure supports quantitative analysis of anisotropic phase evolution and strain partitioning during plastic deformation of the studied steel.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of the undeformed low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel after 850 °C intercritical annealing for 10 min followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) is the 3D reconstructed phase + image quality (IQ) map across three orthogonal planes (RD: rolling direction, TD: transverse direction, ND: normal direction), showing a banded structure along RD: white BCC phases (61.2% total, including α and δ ferrite) and cyan FCC austenite (38.8%), with a scale of 100 μm. Subfigure (b) is the magnified phase + IQ map of the RD-TD plane (scale 5 μm), confirming uniform BCC contrast that indicates no pre-existing martensite, with distinguishable coarse δ ferrite, ultrafine α ferrite and ultrafine austenite. Subfigure (c) is the 3D reconstructed inverse pole figure orientation map showing grain orientation distribution, with a scale of 100 μm. Subfigures (d) and (e) show grain size distributions: austenite has an average grain size of ~1.8 μm, α ferrite ~2 μm, and coarse δ ferrite ~38 μm, presenting a bimodal grain size distribution. The results verify the dual-scale banded microstructure of the annealed steel, supporting subsequent anisotropic deformation mechanism analysis.

This figure presents room-temperature tensile mechanical properties of intercritically annealed low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel tested along rolling direction (RD, red curves) and transverse direction (TD, blue curves). Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, with x-axis as engineering strain (%) and y-axis as engineering stress (MPa). The RD sample exhibits higher ultimate tensile strength (864 MPa) and total elongation (69%) than the TD sample, while the overall steel achieves a good combination of ~855±35 MPa tensile strength and 65±5% total elongation. Subplot (b) presents corresponding true stress-strain curves and strain hardening rate curves, with x-axis as true strain and y-axis as strain hardening rate (dσ/dε) and true stress (MPa). The strain hardening behavior is divided into three stages: Stage I features a sharp drop of strain hardening rate; Stage II starts at ~0.2 true strain with rising strain hardening rate; Stage III shows decreasing strain hardening rate until necking. The maximum strain hardening rate of the RD sample (2052 MPa) is higher than that of the TD sample (1828 MPa), which explains the anisotropic mechanical performance of the steel, supporting that the observed mechanical anisotropy arises from different strain-induced martensite transformation kinetics under the two loading directions.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of a Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al low-density medium manganese steel transverse direction (TD) sample strained to 15% engineering tension. Subplot (a) is a phase + band slope (BS) map, where green denotes FCC austenite, red gradient (BS 20 to 255) denotes BCC phases, showing microstructure consisting of coarse banded δ-ferrite and ultrafine γ+α aggregate regions. Subplot (b) shows a bimodal frequency distribution of BCC BS values, with lower BS peak (~110) corresponding to α'-martensite and higher BS peak (~180) corresponding to ferrite; their intersection is used as the threshold to separate the two BCC phases. Subplot (c) is the kernel average misorientation (KAM) map (0° to 3° scale) for region (a), showing higher KAM (higher local dislocation density) in the ultrafine γ+α region than δ-ferrite. Subplot (d) is the magnified phase map of the dashed box in (a), identifying α'-martensite (grey), ferrite (red) and austenite (green), revealing α' preferentially nucleates at austenite/ferrite phase boundaries. Subplot (e) is the FCC KAM map of region (d), with arrows marking high misorientation at phase boundaries, confirming strain concentration at these α' nucleation sites. The figure supports that strain-induced α'-martensite nucleation at phase boundaries, driven by local strain and high geometrically necessary dislocation density, contributes to the alloy's TRIP effect and strain hardening capability.

This set of TEM characterization is performed on transverse direction (TD) sample of low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel deformed to 15% engineering strain. Subfigures a₁ and b₁ are bright field (BF) TEM images, a₂ and b₂ are corresponding dark field (DF) TEM images, with a uniform scale bar of 500 nm. The attached selected area electron diffraction patterns identify FCC structured austenite (γ, zone axis Z=[011]) and BCC structured α'-martensite (zone axis Z=[-111]). Observations directly reveal that strain-induced α'-martensite preferentially nucleates adjacent to γ/α ferrite and γ/δ ferrite phase boundaries. This figure provides direct microstructural evidence for the phase boundary nucleation mechanism of strain-induced martensitic transformation, which contributes to the transformation-induced plasticity effect of the studied steel.

This set of TEM images characterize the deformation microstructures of a transverse direction (TD) low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al) sample deformed to 15% engineering strain. Subfigure a1 presents a bright-field (BF) TEM image and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of FCC austenite (γ) grain, revealing the formation of stacking faults (SFs, marked by red arrows). Subfigure a2 is the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image taken from the SF region in a1, showing atomic-scale stacking sequence features of SFs. Subfigures b1-b2 show BF TEM and SAED pattern of BCO δ-ferrite grain, where high-density dislocation walls and dislocation tangles are observed. Subfigures c1-c2 show BF TEM and SAED pattern of BCC α-ferrite grain, also containing dense dislocation tangles. The figure demonstrates distinct deformation mechanisms across phases: austenite with lower stacking fault energy undergoes planar slip and SF formation, while both α and δ ferrite experience dislocation cross-slip to form dislocation tangles, supporting the conclusion that deformation mode difference arises from the stacking fault energy discrepancy between austenite and ferrite phases.

This figure investigates the anisotropic deformation behavior of a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel loaded along rolling direction (RD, red curve) and transverse direction (TD, blue curve). Subfigure (a) presents in-situ high-energy XRD patterns at true strains of 0.14, 0.22, 0.3 and 0.38, with FCC austenite peaks (Y₃₁₁, Y₂₂₂, orange diamond markers) and BCC/BCT ferrite/martensite peaks (α₂₁₁, α₂₂₀, green dot markers) marked. FCC peak intensity decreases with increasing strain for both samples, while BCC peaks of RD are higher than TD at high strains, indicating more strain-induced α'-martensite formation in RD. Subfigure (b) shows martensite fraction quantified via Rietveld refinement as a function of true strain, with an inset of corresponding strain hardening rate curves. RD exhibits faster martensite transformation kinetics, with ~4 vol.% higher martensite fraction than TD at true strain of 0.48, corresponding to its higher strain hardening ability. This figure confirms that anisotropic strain-induced martensite transformation is the main origin of the anisotropic mechanical performance of the studied steel.

This figure presents characterization results of a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel for samples loaded along rolling direction (RD, red) and transverse direction (TD, blue). Subplot (a) shows linear relationships between ΔK-βW_hkl and KC^1/2 calculated via the modified Williamson-Hall method for five FCC austenite crystallographic reflections ({111}, {200}, {220}, {311}, {222}) at true strains of 0.14 and 0.22. RD samples exhibit higher linear slopes than TD samples at both strains, corresponding to higher dislocation density. Subplot (b) plots austenite dislocation density as a function of true strain: dislocation density increases with rising true strain for both samples, while RD samples consistently show higher austenite dislocation density (maximum difference ~1.51×10^14 m⁻²), with the gap narrowing at high true strain above 0.35. This figure confirms anisotropic dislocation evolution during deformation, which is attributed to differences in strain-induced martensitic transformation behavior and strain partitioning between RD and TD samples, supporting the core conclusion of anisotropic deformation behavior in the tested steel.

This figure characterizes the strain partitioning behavior of a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel sample tested along the rolling direction (RD). (a₁) is the EBSD phase + band contrast (BC) map of the entire probed area (scale bar 100 μm), with an ultrafine grained (UFG) γ+α region marked by red frame; (b₁) is the high-magnification EBSD phase + BC map of the marked UFG region (scale bar 5 μm), while (c₁) and (d₁) are separated FCC (austenite/γ) and BCC (ferrite/α, δ) phase maps of (b₁), respectively. Subfigures (a₂-a₅), (b₂-b₅), (c₂-c₅), (d₂-d₅) show the shear strain distribution (color scale from blue (low strain) to red (high strain)) of the corresponding regions at engineering strains of 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%, respectively. (e₁) and (e₂) plot the local shear strain variation across δ ferrite and γ+α phase regions, showing strain values of 10-18% with minor discontinuity across the phase boundary. These results confirm obvious strain partitioning between different phases and microregions, indicating that interface strain concentration facilitates strain-induced martensitic transformation during tensile deformation.

This figure illustrates the strain partitioning behavior of the transverse direction (TD) sample of Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al low-density medium manganese steel. It includes EBSD phase + band contrast maps for the full probed area, the ultrafine grain (UFG) γ+α region, and single-phase maps for FCC (austenite/γ) and BCC (ferrite, including δ and α phases). Shear strain distributions of the aforementioned regions at engineering strains of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20% are provided to show strain evolution with increasing deformation. Line plots quantify local shear strain across representative phase regions: higher shear strain (20% to 30%) is concentrated in BCC δ-ferrite, while the UFG γ+α mixed region exhibits lower, more heterogeneous strain levels (mostly 2% to 18%). This figure supports that the TD sample has pronounced strain heterogeneity, with preferential strain localization in δ-ferrite, which accounts for its anisotropic deformation performance compared to rolling direction samples.

This group of four plots presents the average shear strain (unit: %) as a function of engineering strain (range: 4% to 20%, unit: %) for different phases in the low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel tested along rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). The first two subplots compare average shear strain between δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates: similar strain values are observed for the two phases in RD samples, while δ-ferrite shows ~1.26 times higher average shear strain than γ+α aggregates in TD samples, indicating strain concentration in δ-ferrite under TD loading. The last two subplots compare strain within UFG regions: for both RD and TD samples, the γ-α' mixed phase bears ~1.3 times higher average shear strain than α ferrite, and the γ-α' phase in RD samples shows ~15% higher strain than that in TD samples. All curves increase linearly with rising engineering strain, with error bars indicating data deviation. These results demonstrate loading direction-dependent strain partitioning between phases: higher strain concentration in δ-ferrite reduces austenite strain in TD samples, leading to slower strain-induced martensite transformation kinetics and lower strain hardening capability compared to RD samples.

This group of four plots presents lattice strain evolution as a function of applied engineering strain for a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel, measured via in-situ synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction during uniaxial tension along rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Subplots (a) and (b) display lattice strain of individual crystallographic planes for FCC austenite (five planes: (111), (200), (220), (311), (222)) and BCC ferrite (four planes: (110), (200), (211), (220)) for RD and TD samples, respectively. Subplots (c) and (d) show weighted average lattice strains for FCC and BCC phases in RD and TD samples, with arrows marking the engineering strain where curves deviate from linearity, indicating the onset of plastic deformation. Key observations include earlier deviation from linearity for austenite than ferrite in both orientations: austenite plasticity onset occurs at 1.8% (RD) and 2.1% (TD) strain, lower than ferrite onset at 2.3% (RD) and 2.9% (TD). Post-yield, austenite average lattice strain slightly decreases, indicating stress relaxation. These results confirm austenite yields first in this alloy, explaining the higher strain concentration in austenite-containing regions during deformation.

This plot presents the deformation-induced martensitic transformation kinetics of a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel under uniaxial tension along two directions. The x-axis is true strain ranging from 0 to 0.5, and the y-axis is martensite fraction in percentage ranging from 0 to 30. Red open circles represent experimental data for samples loaded along the rolling direction (RD), blue open circles represent experimental data for samples loaded along the transverse direction (TD), and black dashed lines are fitting results based on the Olson-Cohen (OC) model. Martensite fraction increases monotonically with increasing true strain for both directions, and RD samples show consistently higher martensite fraction at the same true strain, indicating faster transformation kinetics in RD. The OC model predictions fit well with experimental data for both directions, verifying that loading direction-dependent strain distribution dominates the anisotropic strain-induced martensite transformation kinetics of the studied steel.

This figure presents local shear strain distribution near the phase boundary between δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates in the rolling direction (RD) sample of low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel, measured via microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC). The upper and lower rows correspond to two independent representative regions, while each column corresponds to global tensile strain levels of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20% respectively, with a 4 μm scale bar for each subplot. The color bar quantifies shear strain from 0 to 30%, dashed black lines mark the δ/γ+α phase boundaries, and red arrows point to boundary regions. Shear strain at phase boundaries is observed to increase with rising global strain, showing distinct strain concentration at phase interfaces. This evidence supports that strain incompatibility between the two phases with different hardness leads to high local strain and geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at boundaries, which promotes faster strain-induced martensite transformation kinetics in the RD sample.
